---
title: "Plan & Design"
description: "Explore existing resources, write data management plans, design studies, and prepare preregistrations"
phase_order: 1
sidebar: open-research-cycle
page-layout: full
css: orc-styles.css
title-block-style: none
---

<div class="orc-phase-hero-compact">
<div class="orc-hero-cycle phase-1">
{{< include ../images/os-cycle.svg >}}
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-body">
<div class="orc-hero-tagline">Set the foundation for open & reliable research</div>
<div class="orc-hero-pills">
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Explore & Reuse</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Check Legal Frameworks</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Write Research Data Management Plans</span>
<span><i class="bi bi-check2-circle"></i> Design Study</span>
</div>
<div class="orc-hero-checkpoint"><i class="bi bi-people-fill"></i> Checkpoint: Study plan presentation & preregistration</div>
</div>
</div>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-search"></i> Explore and Reuse</summary>

Any resource that inspire you or that you want to reuse and/or adapt, must minimally be cited, using their DOIs if they have one (and otherwise URL with author, date, and time of access) and you must follow the licence and/or usage agreement provided by the authors. A research output (e.g. data, code) without a licence cannot be reused even if they appear publicly online.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Articles

* To come up with a well-founded research question, **review existing literature** on open source registries like [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/). 
* Keep track of your bibliography with an open source **reference manager** like [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/). It can be integrated within RStudio to write reproducible manuscripts seamlessly, formatting your bibliography in any format you want. 


::: {.orc-two-column}

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" alt="LMU OSC logo"></div>
<h4>[Introduction to Zotero](https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/)</h4>
<p>An open-source reference manager that integrates with Word, RStudio, and Google Docs. (1h)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/introduction-to-zotero/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">OSC Tutorial</a>
</div>
:::

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg/250px-OpenAlex_2025_favicon.svg.png" alt="OpenAlex icon"></div>
<h4>[OpenAlex](https://openalex.org/)</h4>
<p>All the world's research, connected and open</p>
<a href="https://openalex.org/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">Recommended</a>
</div>
:::

:::



### Preregistrations

To have some insight into projects that are currently ongoing, you can check for projects that were _preregistered_* and will either get published in the following years or might get accompanied by a note if the project is abandoned. Some registries are discipline-specific while others are discipline-agnostic:_<!-- how to make this a list with  space above it?-->
* [https://osf.io/registries](https://osf.io/registries)
* [https://aspredicted.org/](https://aspredicted.org/)
* [https://preclinicaltrials.eu/](https://preclinicaltrials.eu/)
* [https://clinicaltrials.gov/](https://clinicaltrials.gov/)

*_A preregistration typically consists of a hypothesis and predictions, a plan for data collection (when relevant), and a plan for data analysis, that researchers upload before starting their projects, often in order to increase the rigor of confirmatory research (see '**Study Design & Analysis plan**')_<!-- how to link a tab in this page? -->

#### <i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS & RESOURCES

::: {.orc-grid-tools}

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg/3840px-Open_Science_Framework_log_%28and_Center_for_Open_Science_logo%29.svg.png" alt="OSF"></div>
<h4>[OSF](https://osf.io/registries/)</h4>
<p>Registry of preregistrations. Widely used across fields.</p>
<a href="https://osf.io/registries/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">Recommended</a>
</div>
:::

### Data

To find existing datasets, find discipline-specific repositories on [re3data](https://www.re3data.org/) which is a central registry of all repositories, or explore subject agnostic repositories such as [DataCite](https://datacite.org/) or [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) (oftentimes with the corresponding analysis code). These platforms give you access to existing data or provide metadata and explanations on how to request access to the data.

<!-- add zenodo card-->
::: {.orc-grid-tools}

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><i class="bi bi-database"></i></div>
<h4>[re3data](https://www.re3data.org/)</h4>
<p>Registry of data repositories. Search across disciplines to find domain-specific options.</p>
<a href="https://www.re3data.org/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">Recommended</a>
</div>

:::


### Code

 You may also find code available for reuse on [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) (archived) and/or possibly still in active development on [Github](https://github.com/).

<!-- do we want just the tools or the tutorial here? maybe both? see e.g. card in preserve and share website version -->
<!-- add zenodo card-->
::: {.orc-grid-tools}
<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/github" alt="GitHub"></div>
<h4>[GitHub](https://github.com/) / [GitLab](https://gitlab.com/)</h4>
<p>Version control platforms for code collaboration with issue tracking.</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/Introduction-RStudio-Git-GitHub/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">Tutorial</a>
</div>
:::

:::

</details>


<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-shield-check"></i> Legal Requirements</summary>

::: {.panel-tabset}

### LMU guidelines

3 lines of text



### Funders

3 lines of text



### Ethics

3 lines of text


:::

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-lightbulb"></i> <i class="bi bi-clipboard-check"></i> Research Data Management Plans</summary>

A Data Management Plan (DMP) documents how you will handle research data throughout your project. Write it before your ethics submission, since the content overlaps significantly and most funders require one. Writing a DMP prompts you to think through decisions you might otherwise leave implicit, and documents them using standard terminology.

#### <i class="bi bi-list-check"></i> Your DMP will address:

- **What data** will you collect or generate (types, formats, volume, sources)?
- **How will you describe it** (metadata standards, documentation practices)?
- **How will you organize files** (naming conventions, folder structure, versioning)?
- **Where will you store it** (locations, backups, access controls)?
- **How will you ensure quality** (validation checks, error-handling)?
- **How will you share outputs** (repositories, licenses, embargo periods)?
- **What constraints apply** (consent, anonymization, GDPR, data use agreements)?

The specific questions vary by discipline, data type, and funder requirements. DMP tools like RDMO guide you through the relevant questions with funder-specific templates. Your DMP is a living document: start with what you know, and the rest of the cycle will help you refine the details as your project develops.

**See example DMPs:** [DMPonline public plans](https://dmponline.dcc.ac.uk/public_plans) | [Argos published DMPs](https://argos.openaire.eu/explore-plans) | [RIOjournal DMPs](https://riojournal.com/browse_journal_articles.php?form_name=filter_articles&selfurl=&backurl=&sortby=0&journal_id=17&search_hidden=&search_in_=0&search_in_hidden=&alerts_subject_cats=&alerts_sd498444d0a_publishing_status=&section_type%5B%5D=231)

::: {.orc-two-column}

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-mortarboard"></i> LEARN MORE

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="../../assets/img/lmu_osc_logo.jpg" alt="LMU OSC"></div>
<h4>[FAIR Data Management](https://lmu-osc.github.io/FAIR-Data-Management/)</h4>
<p>Includes a chapter on writing DMPs (2h total)</p>
<a href="https://lmu-osc.github.io/FAIR-Data-Management/dmp.html" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">DMP chapter</a>
</div>
:::

::: {.orc-column}
#### <i class="bi bi-tools"></i> TOOLS

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://rdmo.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_static/RDMO-logo.png" alt="RDMO"></div>
<h4>[RDMO](https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/)</h4>
<p>Funder-compliant templates (DFG, ERC, Horizon Europe)</p>
<a href="https://rdmo.ub.lmu.de/" class="orc-card-tool-badge recommended">LMU supported</a>
</div>
:::

:::

</details>



<details class="orc-details-panel">
<summary><i class="bi bi-graph-up"></i> Study Design & Analysis Plan</summary>

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Pre-analysis planning

3 lines of text

::: {.orc-grid-tools}

<div class="orc-card-tool">
<div class="orc-card-tool-icon"><img src="https://cdn.simpleicons.org/osf" alt="OSF"></div>
<h4>[OSF](https://osf.io/)</h4>
<p>Flexible templates, embargoes, file storage. Widely used across fields.</p>
</div>
:::

### Simulation of Data

3 lines of text



### Power analyses

3 lines of text


:::


</details>

Checklist
